SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a coherent probabilistic log-linear joint model and describes reasonable learning/inference procedures (SampleRank, message-passing, ILP) and reports empirical evidence with statistical tests (paired t-test, p < 0.05). Mathematical presentation of the model (Eq.1) is consistent and the inference/learning choices are appropriate for the stated objective. However, some assumptions are strong (e.g. attachment structure is assumed given during training/testing or heuristically built when unavailable) and certain implementation/hyperparameter details are omitted, leaving minor gaps in reproducibility and assessment of robustness. Overall, the claims are supported by experiments but depend on the stated dataset/attachment assumptions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes joint modeling of phrase-level content selection and discourse-relation prediction in meetings, which departs from prior work that typically uses gold discourse labels as features. While related approaches exist (e.g. DCRF for email threads cited as Oya and Carenini (2014)), the application and explicit joint feature modeling of content and discourse for meetings appears to be a non-trivial and meaningful extension. The paper clearly positions itself against prior work that relied on gold discourse labels and demonstrates a latent-discourse variant, which strengthens the novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (model description, learning, inference, experiments) and presents key equations and feature types clearly (Eq.1, φc/φd/φcd). However, the provided text contains repeated passages and some missing presentation elements (figures/tables referenced but not included here), and several implementation details (exact feature definitions, hyperparameters, parsing/ILP setup) are only summarized, making parts harder to follow or reproduce. Notation is mostly consistent but some descriptions (e.g. clustering of candidates, attachment heuristics) could be expanded for clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments span multiple corpora (AMI-SUB, AMI-FULL, ICSI-FULL) and tasks (phrase selection, discourse labeling, COU prediction) and include comparisons (SVM baselines, Kim and Shah 2016) and statistical testing, showing reasonable breadth. The paper explores joint vs separate learning and a latent-discourse variant, but detailed ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g. impact of attachment heuristics, hyperparameters, failure cases) and reproduction-level details are limited in the provided text. Discussion of limitations is minimal, so the experimental depth is adequate but not exhaustive.

IMPACT.justification: The work addresses an important problem—identifying salient content and discourse relations in meetings—which is useful for summarization and understanding (authors cite applications to abstractive summarization and COU prediction). The demonstrated improvements over baselines and a state-of-the-art COU method suggest practical relevance, but the approach is an incremental advance rather than transformative and its broader adoption may depend on handling attachment assumptions and scalability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—joint discourse and content modeling for dialogue/meeting analysis—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences and targets the appropriate audience. Methods (log-linear modeling, SampleRank, message-passing, ILP) and evaluations (ROUGE, classification F1, statistical tests) align with venue standards, and the paper positions itself within relevant prior work and datasets (AMI, ICSI).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against reasonable baselines (SVM-based classifiers) and a cited state-of-the-art method for COU (Kim and Shah 2016), and reports statistical significance for improvements. Comparisons use the same corpora and standard metrics (ROUGE, F1) as prior work, and the paper discusses joint vs separate learning to isolate contributions. Some recent or neural baselines are not discussed in the provided text and more detailed analysis of when baselines perform comparably would strengthen the comparisons.
